Extending McFarland’s cross-trial decomposition to non-uniform trials and non-homogeneous stimuli

A methodological note

fem-v1-fovea methods note

2026-05-29

Summary

McFarland, Cumming & Butts (2016) introduced a cross-trial, eye-position-conditioned decomposition that separates the stimulus-driven and stimulus-independent components of V1 responses in the presence of fixational eye movements (FEMs). It is now the standard estimator for the FEM fraction 1−α1-\alpha, the corrected noise covariance CnoisecorrC_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}}, and the Fano factor under FEMs. Two assumptions sit inside it:

  • (A1) Uniform trial/time-bin structure — every analysis time bin tt has the same number of trials ntn_t, so the across-time-bin weighting is the same for every term in the law-of-total-(co)variance (LOTC).
  • (A2) Statistically stationary stimulus — the across-time-bin distribution of r(t,e)r(t,e) at a fixed eye position is the same at every ee (equivalently, 𝔼t[rk(t,e)]\mathbb{E}_t[r^k(t,e)] is independent of ee for the moments used). It then does not matter which eye-position distribution the rate moments are averaged over, and the close-pair restriction in (7) is free.

Both assumptions fail in the structured fixRSVP stimulus used here. Fixation durations vary across trials, so the per-time-bin trial count ntn_t drops across time bins; and the windowed natural-image stimulus is far from translation-invariant, so the rate r(t,e)r(t,e) depends on absolute eye position. This note develops, validates, and discusses a methodological extension of McFarland et al. for each assumption violation:

  1. Consistent time-bin weighting under variable ntn_t. The close-pair rate estimator is intrinsically pair-count weighted (∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2) across time bins; for the LOTC to hold term-by-term, the PSTH covariance and the mean entering the rate-variance subtraction must use the same pair-count weighting. Validated against closed-form synthetic ground truth with variable per-time-bin trial counts.
  2. Eye-position-distribution matching under a non-homogeneous stimulus. Close pairs at threshold Δe<ε\Delta e<\varepsilon are sampled in proportion to the squared eye-position density p(e)2p(e)^2, while the total covariance and the PSTH covariance are over p(e)p(e). We restore consistency with an importance-reweighted estimator that has the target eye distribution as a parameter, with two principled choices (pp and p2p^2). Their gap measures whether the rate has spatial structure on the fixation scale — non-zero even under (A2) when ℓ∼σ\ell\sim\sigma.

Sections 1 and 2 set up McFarland’s estimator and the synthetic model that breaks both assumptions. Sections 3 and 4 develop and validate one extension each on the synthetic. Section 5 reports the consequences on synthetic and real data. Section 6 records the production-pipeline state — Extension 1 has already been integrated; Extension 2 is implemented in this folder and gated.

1. Background: the cross-trial decomposition under FEMs

1.1 The law of total (co)variance

Let Yci(t)Y_c^i(t) be the spike count of neuron cc on trial ii at analysis time bin tt (a frozen-stimulus time bin). Each trial samples an absolute eye position ee from the fixational distribution p(e)p(e); we treat ee as approximately constant over the short counting window and approximately independent of tt. Writing the firing rate as rc(t,e)=𝔼[Yc∣t,e]r_c(t,e) = \mathbb{E}[Y_c \mid t, e], the law of total variance partitions the stimulus-driven rate variance into a stimulus-locked (PSTH) part and an eye-movement (FEM) part:

Vart,e∼p[rc(t,e)]⏟total rate variance=Vart[𝔼e∼p[rc∣t]]⏟PSTH variance+𝔼t[Vare∼p[rc∣t]]⏟FEM variance. \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}_{t,\,e\sim p}\!\big[r_c(t,e)\big]}_{\text{total rate variance}} \;=\; \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}_t\!\Big[\,\mathbb{E}_{e\sim p}\!\big[r_c \mid t\big]\Big]}_{\text{PSTH variance}} \;+\; \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_t\!\Big[\,\mathrm{Var}_{e\sim p}\!\big[r_c \mid t\big]\Big]}_{\text{FEM variance}} . \tag{1}

McFarland et al. define the fraction of stimulus-driven variance captured by the PSTH,

αc=Vart[𝔼e∼p[rc∣t]]Vart,e∼p[rc],1−αc=FEM fraction. \alpha_c \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{Var}_t\big[\mathbb{E}_{e\sim p}[r_c\mid t]\big]} {\mathrm{Var}_{t,e\sim p}[r_c]} , \qquad 1-\alpha_c \;=\; \text{FEM fraction}. \tag{2}

The pairwise analogue partitions the count covariance, Covt,e∼p[Ym,Yn]=Covt,e∼p[rm,rn]+𝔼[noise cov]\mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[Y_m,Y_n] = \mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[r_m,r_n] + \mathbb{E}\big[\text{noise cov}\big], so the FEM-corrected (stimulus-independent) covariance is

Cnoisecorr=Ctotal−Crate,Fanoc=(Cnoisecorr)ccr‾c, C_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}} \;=\; C_{\text{total}} \;-\; C_{\text{rate}}, \qquad \text{Fano}_c \;=\; \frac{\big(C_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}}\big)_{cc}}{\bar r_c}, \tag{3}

where Ctotal=Covt,e∼p[Y]C_{\text{total}} = \mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[Y] is the raw count covariance and Crate=Covt,e∼p[r]C_{\text{rate}} = \mathrm{Cov}_{t,e\sim p}[r] is the total rate covariance over the same distribution pp. Equation (3) is only meaningful if CrateC_{\text{rate}} and CtotalC_{\text{total}} are measured over one and the same eye-position distribution; this is the consistency requirement that the rest of this note is about.

1.2 The cross-trial trick: distinct trials cancel the observation noise

We never observe rc(t,e)r_c(t,e) directly — only the noisy counts YcY_c. The engine of the whole method is that the observation noise is independent across distinct trials, so for two different trials i≠ji\neq j at the same time bin tt,

𝔼[Yci(t)Ycj(t)|ei,ej]=rc(t,ei)rc(t,ej). \mathbb{E}\big[Y_c^i(t)\,Y_c^j(t)\,\big|\,e_i,e_j\big] = r_c(t,e_i)\,r_c(t,e_j). \tag{4}

The product of two distinct trials’ counts is an unbiased estimate of the product of their underlying rates, with the Poisson/observation variance removed. Every estimator below is a different way of averaging these distinct-trial products — and the choice of which pairs to average sets the eye-position distribution.

1.3 Two families of estimator

Signal (PSTH) variance and covariance — all distinct pairs. Averaging (4) over all distinct pairs at the same time bin, then over time bins, estimates the PSTH (stimulus-locked) second moment (McFarland’s Eq. 6):

Vart[𝔼irci(t)]̂=⟨⟨Yci(t)Ycj(t)⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾c2, \widehat{\mathrm{Var}_t\!\big[\mathbb E_i\, r^i_c(t)\big]} = \big\langle \langle Y_c^i(t)\,Y_c^j(t)\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_c^{\,2}, \tag{5} Covt[𝔼irmi,𝔼irni]̂=⟨⟨Ymi(t)Ynj(t)⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾mY‾n. \widehat{\mathrm{Cov}_t\!\big[\mathbb E_i\, r^i_m,\ \mathbb E_i\, r^i_n\big]} = \big\langle \langle Y_m^i(t)\,Y_n^j(t)\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_m\,\bar Y_n. \tag{6}

Because all same-time-bin pairs are used, the per-bin mean averages over the full fixational distribution p(e)p(e): these terms live on p(e)p(e).

Eye-conditioned rate variance and covariance — close pairs only. Restrict the same averages to pairs whose eye trajectories nearly coincide, Δeij<ε\Delta e_{ij}<\varepsilon. This drives ei≈ej≈ee_i\approx e_j\approx e, so (4) →rc(t,e)2\to r_c(t,e)^2: the total rate second moment with the Poisson noise removed (their Eqs. 8 and 16),

Vari,t[rc]̂=⟨⟨Yci(t)Ycj(t)∣Δeij<ε⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾c2, \widehat{\mathrm{Var}_{i,t}[r_c]} = \big\langle \langle Y_c^i(t)\,Y_c^j(t)\mid \Delta e_{ij}<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_c^{\,2}, \tag{7} Covi,t[rm,rn]̂=⟨⟨Ymi(t)Ynj(t)∣Δeij<ε⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾mY‾n. \widehat{\mathrm{Cov}_{i,t}[r_m,r_n]} = \big\langle \langle Y_m^i(t)\,Y_n^j(t)\mid \Delta e_{ij}<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\big\rangle_t - \bar Y_m\,\bar Y_n. \tag{8}

The close-pair restriction is exactly what cancels the Poisson noise across distinct trials — but, as §4 shows, it also silently changes the eye-position distribution these terms live on.

1.4 The reported quantities

McFarland’s reported quantities combine the two estimator families. The FEM fraction is αc=(5)/(7)\alpha_c = (5)/(7) (Eq. 10); the corrected noise covariance and Fano factor are Cnoisecorr=Ctotal−CrateC_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}} = C_{\text{total}} - C_{\text{rate}} from (3) with the close-pair CrateC_{\text{rate}} from (8). McFarland et al. also give an analytical form for α\alpha via the Fourier transform of pp (their Eq. 9), in which α\alpha weights the rate spectrum |R(k)|2|R(k)|^2 by the eye-distribution spectrum |P(k)|2|P(k)|^2.

1.5 Three estimators on two weighting axes

Each estimator above averages distinct-trial products in a different way. Reading directly off (5)–(8) and the LOTC LHS of (1), each lives on an implicit weighting in two dimensions — across analysis time bins (wtw_t) and across eye positions (qq):

Estimator implicit wtw_t (across-bin) implicit qq (across-eye)
CtotalC_\text{total} — LHS of (1); sample variance over all (i,t)(i,t) ∝nt\propto n_t (trial-count) pp
CpsthC_\text{psth} — Eq. (6): ⟨⟨YiYj⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾Y‾⊤\langle\langle Y^i Y^j\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t - \bar Y\bar Y^\top 1/T1/T for MM; ∝nt\propto n_t for Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top pp
CrateC_\text{rate} — Eq. (8): ⟨⟨YiYj∣Δe<ε⟩i≠j⟩t−Y‾Y‾⊤\langle\langle Y^i Y^j \mid \Delta e<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t - \bar Y\bar Y^\top 1/T1/T for MM; ∝nt\propto n_t for Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top p2p^2 for MM; pp for Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top

The LOTC (1) holds term-by-term only when all three estimators land on a single (wt,q)(w_t, q). The literal McFarland forms do not: even inside one estimator the second-moment and the mean-square subtractor disagree. McFarland’s nested-bracket notation ⟨⟨⋅⟩i≠j⟩t\langle\langle\,\cdot\,\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t averages within bin tt first and then averages bins uniformly, putting MM at wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T; the Y‾\bar Y that enters Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top is the global mean over (i,t)(i, t) and pools samples uniformly, putting that term at wt∝ntw_t \propto n_t. And CrateC_\text{rate}’s MM is the only cell at q=p2q = p^2 (the close-pair conditional density, §4.1); every other cell is at q=pq = p.

Two assumptions in McFarland’s regime make these inconsistencies invisible:

  • (A1) Uniform trial/time-bin structure. When ntn_t is constant, ∝nt\propto n_t and 1/T1/T coincide up to a global scaling, so the wtw_t column collapses to a single value across all three estimators (and across the MM / Y‾\bar Y split within each).

  • (A2) Statistically stationary stimulus. The across-time-bin distribution of r(t,e)r(t,e) at a fixed eye position is the same at every ee, i.e. 𝔼t[rk(t,e)]\mathbb{E}_t[r^k(t,e)] is independent of ee for the moments used. Then 𝔼e∼D[𝔼t[rk(t,e)]]\mathbb{E}_{e\sim D}\!\big[\mathbb E_t[r^k(t,e)]\big] is the same for every distribution DD, so the qq column collapses: the close-pair restriction in (7)–(8) — which silently sets D=p2D = p^2 — gives the same answer as the actual viewing target D=pD = p. McFarland et al. state the assumption at the level of the stimulus (their text around Eqs. M7–M10):

    “by restricting analysis to trial pairs where Δeij≈0\Delta e_{ij}\approx 0, [the estimator] gives an estimate of 𝔼[r2(e,t)]\mathbb{E}[r^2(e,t)] under the conditional eye position distribution p(e∣Δe≈0)p(e\mid\Delta e\approx 0) rather than p(e)p(e). However, for a stimulus that is statistically invariant to spatial translations (such as used in our study), the expectation of r2(e,t)r^2(e,t) with respect to any distribution over ee will be the same.”

    Stimulus stationarity is sufficient: when each frame is a sample from a translation-invariant random field, the rate distribution at one eye position is, in distribution, the rate distribution at any other, so the across-time-bin moments at fixed ee do not depend on ee. McFarland’s ternary bar noise has this property; the structured fixRSVP images used here do not.

In McFarland’s regime — (A1) ∩ (A2) — every cell of the table collapses to a single (wt,q)(w_t, q), the three estimators agree, and (1) reduces to the textbook LOTC. Outside that regime each axis fails on its own and the LOTC fails term-by-term. §3 develops two consistent choices on the wtw_t axis under (A1) failure; §4 develops two consistent choices on the qq axis under (A2) failure. The two extensions are orthogonal — each fills one column of the table — and §2 makes both failures concrete on the fixRSVP stimulus.

2. Our setting and the synthetic model

2.1 Violation of (A1): uneven fixation durations ⇒\Rightarrow variable ntn_t

fixRSVP trials are organized around fixational fixation periods of variable duration. Aligned to fixation onset and binned into analysis time bins, this gives a monotonically decreasing per-time-bin trial count: every trial is fixating in early bins, but only long-fixation trials contribute to late bins. On a representative session (Allen 2022-04-13, 49 cells) ntn_t ranged from 42 to 78 across time bins (CV ≈0.19\approx 0.19); on our broader set of cells the spread is larger. Under this ntn_t variation, the close-pair rate estimator weights each time bin by nt(nt−1)/2n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 pairs while a uniform split-half PSTH estimator weights each time bin by 1/T1/T — these only coincide if ntn_t is constant.

2.2 Violation of (A2): the windowed fixRSVP stimulus

fixRSVP presents a natural image at a fixed location on the screen, behind a spatial window (aperture). The neuron’s receptive field (RF) is fixed in retinotopic coordinates, so in screen coordinates it moves with the eye. Two mechanisms make the across-time-bin distribution of rc(t,e)r_c(t,e) at fixed eye position depend on absolute eye position — directly violating (A2):

  1. Windowing — a hard non-stationarity. Once the fixation offset is large enough to carry the RF off the windowed image, the RF samples only the uniform gray background and the rate collapses to the gray-screen baseline regardless of analysis time bin tt. So at peripheral ee the across-time-bin distribution of rr is concentrated at baseline; at central ee it is strongly stimulus-modulated. The across-time-bin moments at fixed ee are therefore strongly ee-dependent — before any image content is considered.
  2. Image structure. Within the window, drift slides the structured image across the RF, so which feature drives the cell — and how strongly — depends on absolute eye position. The across-time-bin distribution of rr at each ee samples a different patch of a fixed image, not different draws from a translation-invariant ensemble; the per-ee moments differ accordingly.

2.3 A unified generative model that breaks both assumptions

We validate every estimator claim below against a single synthetic generator (synthetic.py) chosen so that (A1) and (A2) are switches on top of one architecture. With both switches off, the generator sits squarely in McFarland’s regime, with a closed-form 1−αp1-\alpha^p that depends on a single ratio — the rate’s spatial scale relative to the fixation scale — and covers (0,1)(0,1) as that ratio is swept.

Rate field. For neuron cc, analysis time bin tt, and absolute eye position e∈ℝ2e\in\mathbb R^2,

rc(t,e)=μ0+Mc(e)α(t)st(e), r_c(t,e) \;=\; \mu_0 \;+\; M_c(e)\,\alpha(t)\,s_t(e), \tag{9}

with three separable ingredients:

  • st(⋅)s_t(\cdot) — a per-time-bin i.i.d. draw of a stationary 2-D zero-mean Gaussian random field with covariance K(δ)=τ2exp(−∥δ∥2/(2ℓ2))K(\delta) = \tau^2\exp\!\big(-\lVert\delta\rVert^2/(2\ell^2)\big). This is the rate map at time bin tt. Independent time bins give the standard cross-trial cancellation of observation noise. At any fixed ee, st(e)∼𝒩(0,τ2)s_t(e)\sim\mathcal N(0,\tau^2) across time bins — the across-time-bin distribution is independent of ee, so the field component is (A2) by construction.
  • α(t)\alpha(t) — a per-time-bin amplitude envelope. Default α≡1\alpha\equiv 1. When the synthetic targets Extension 1 (§3) we set α(t)\alpha(t) to decay across time bins, mirroring fixRSVP’s onset transients (high amplitude in early, high-ntn_t time bins). Without correlation between α\alpha and ntn_t, the Extension-1 bias washes out.
  • Mc(e)M_c(e) — the per-cell spatial mask in [0,1][0,1], the (A2) switch. Physically, Mc(e)M_c(e) is the fraction of the windowed stimulus the cell sees at eye position ee: when the RF leaves the window, M(e)→0M(e)\to 0 and the rate collapses to baseline regardless of time bin, which is precisely §2.2’s “hard windowing” mechanism. M≡1M\equiv 1 recovers a fully homogeneous stimulus; any non-constant MM breaks (A2) at the second moment: 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]=μ02+M(e)2𝔼t[α2]τ2\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)]=\mu_0^2 + M(e)^2\,\mathbb E_t[\alpha^2]\,\tau^2, which depends on ee through M(e)2M(e)^2 — the exact form McFarland’s caveat is about (their text around Eqs. M7–M10). The first moment 𝔼t[r(t,e)]=μ0\mathbb E_t[r(t,e)] = \mu_0 stays ee-independent.

Four mask shapes span the relevant cases:

mask kind Mc(e)M_c(e) physical role
flat 11 homogeneous stimulus — (A2) holds
central exp(−∥e∥2/(2ℓM2))\exp\!\big(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\ell_M^2)\big) windowed stimulus: cell sees the stimulus near fixation, only baseline in periphery
eccentric 1−exp(−∥e∥2/(2ℓM2))1 - \exp\!\big(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\ell_M^2)\big) the bounded complement — cell suppressed at fixation, sees the stimulus when eye drifts
linear 12(1+tanh(x/ℓM))\tfrac12\big(1+\tanh(x/\ell_M)\big) smooth bounded xx-gradient — the simplest unidirectional non-homogeneity

central is the workhorse stand-in for the windowed fixRSVP mechanism. flat is the regime in which McFarland’s estimator is unbiased; we use it in §2.4 to sanity-check the estimator against a non-trivial analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p that the additive model could not provide. eccentric and linear stress-test the corrected estimator on masks whose variance lives where close pairs are rare or biased.

Eye distribution. Eyes are drawn i.i.d. e∼p=𝒩(0,σ2I)e\sim p=\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I) per (trial, time-bin), σ=0.15∘\sigma=0.15^\circ (realistic fixational drift). For an isotropic Gaussian pp, the close-pair density p(e)2p(e)^2 is exactly 𝒩(0,σ2/2I)\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2/2\,I) — a tighter Gaussian with half the variance — so the LOTC under pp or p2p^2 has a closed form (Appendix §A.5).

Trial structure. The (trial, time-bin) array has shape (Ntr,T)(N_{\text{tr}}, T). With ntr/bin=Nonen_{\text{tr/bin}}=\text{None} every time bin has NtrN_{\text{tr}} trials, recovering McFarland’s uniform-trial regime. To violate (A1) we set ntr/bin=(nt)t=1Tn_{\text{tr/bin}}=(n_t)_{t=1}^{T} — a monotonically decaying staircase (default lo 1515, hi ∼Ntr\sim N_{\text{tr}}, see Fig. 1A) that mimics the fixation-duration distribution. Entries beyond ntn_t in each time bin are masked invalid.

Observation noise. Spikes are drawn Yc∼Poisson(rc(t,e))Y_c \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(r_c(t,e)) (optionally with a shared latent giving a known stimulus-independent noise covariance). The rate is marginally 𝒩(μ0,M(e)2τ2)\mathcal N(\mu_0, M(e)^2\tau^2); with μ0=6,τ≤1\mu_0=6,\,\tau\leq 1, Pr[r<0]∼10−9\Pr[r<0]\sim 10^{-9} and the clip to r≥10−6r\geq 10^{-6} effectively never triggers. An exponential link is the standard alternative but obscures the closed form.

Stimulus-frame caveat. The synthetic treats each analysis time bin tt as an independent fresh draw of the field sts_t, mirroring McFarland’s “stimulus phase” abstraction. In real fixRSVP this is an idealization: the natural-image stimulus refreshes at 2020 Hz while the analysis typically operates at 6060 or 120120 Hz, so several consecutive analysis time bins fall within the same 5050 ms stimulus-frame interval and share the same underlying sts_t — they are highly correlated, not i.i.d. Across stimulus-frame boundaries the rate is genuinely refreshed. The practical consequence (developed in §A.6) is that the effective TT controlling the across-bin SEM floor is closer to #fixations×#stim-frames per fixation\#\text{fixations}\times\#\text{stim-frames per fixation} than the raw bin count #fixations×#bins per fixation\#\text{fixations}\times\#\text{bins per fixation}. Within- stimulus-frame reliability — how the cell’s response varies across the several analysis bins of one frame — is a future direction; the analyses below treat this as out-of-scope and use the i.i.d.-bin synthetic as a clean reference for the McFarland-style estimator.

Closed-form ground truth. With MM depending only on ee and α\alpha only on tt, and the field zero-mean, the LOTC decomposition admits a closed form under any distribution DD over ee and any time-bin weighting wtw_t (derivation in Appendix §A.5):

VartotalD,w=𝔼w[α2]τ2𝔼D[M2],VarpsthD,w=𝔼w[α2]IM,K,D, \mathrm{Var}_{\text{total}}^{D,w} = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2], \qquad \mathrm{Var}_{\text{psth}}^{D,w} = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,I_{M,K,D}, \tag{10}

with IM,K,D=∬M(e1)M(e2)K(e1−e2)D(e1)D(e2)de1de2I_{M,K,D}=\iint M(e_1) M(e_2)\,K(e_1{-}e_2)\,D(e_1)\,D(e_2)\,de_1\,de_2. The ratio 1−αD,w=1−IM,K,D/(τ2𝔼D[M2])1-\alpha^{D,w} = 1 - I_{M,K,D}/(\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]) is invariant under time-bin weighting: the envelope factor cancels. Both wtw_t directions of Extension 1 therefore target the same truth on the ratio; they differ only in finite-sample efficiency (§3.3).

For the flat mask and Gaussian D,KD, K, the integral closes analytically:

1−αp=2σ2ℓ2+2σ2,1−αp2=σ2ℓ2+σ2, 1-\alpha^p = \frac{2\sigma^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma^2}, \qquad 1-\alpha^{p^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{\ell^2 + \sigma^2}, \tag{11}

a single-parameter family in ℓ/σ\ell/\sigma that covers (0,1)(0,1). For central (Gaussian) the closed form is also available; for eccentric and linear we use Monte Carlo (4M-sample default, sampling noise ≲10−3\lesssim 10^{-3}).

2.4 McFarland’s estimator recovers analytical 1−αp1-\alpha^p under (A1)+(A2)

The unified model lets us test McFarland’s estimator in his native regime against a closed-form 1−αp1-\alpha^p that covers (0,1)(0,1) — something the purely additive synthetic could not do, because there the only (A2)-respecting profile was F≡constF\equiv\mathrm{const}, which forced 1−α=01-\alpha=0. Setting kinds=['flat'] and varying ℓ/σ\ell/\sigma traces the full 1−αp1-\alpha^p axis.

The empirical estimator is decompose(target='naive', time_bin_weighting='pair_count') with constant ntn_t — the same shape of close-pair estimator McFarland’s paper specifies. Under (A2), 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)] is independent of ee (the field component is stationary, and the mask is constant), so the close-pair second moment converges to 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)] at any ee — and in particular to the same value τ2+μ02\tau^2 + \mu_0^2 regardless of which density samples the close-pair midpoints. With Y‾→μ0\bar Y \to \mu_0, Crate→τ2=VartotalpC_\text{rate}\to\tau^2 = \mathrm{Var}_\text{total}^p and CpsthC_\text{psth} (split-half over pp) →τ2ℓ2/(ℓ2+2σ2)\to\tau^2\ell^2/(\ell^2+2\sigma^2), giving 1−α→2σ2/(ℓ2+2σ2)=1−αp1-\alpha\to 2\sigma^2/(\ell^2+2\sigma^2) = 1-\alpha^p. So McFarland is unbiased for 1−αp1-\alpha^p — even though the close-pair density itself is intrinsically p2p^2, not pp.

Figure 0 confirms this empirically across an ℓ/σ\ell/\sigma sweep and shows the analytical closed-form gap as a function of ℓ/σ\ell/\sigma; the gap is non-zero under (A2), peaking at ℓ≈σ\ell\approx\sigma. This is the reframing §4.5 builds on: the gap is a fixation-scale spatial-structure measure, not an (A2) test.

The estimator’s consistency (how the seed-to-seed SEM depends on the trials-per-time-bin nn and the number of time bins TT) is more subtle than 1/n1/\sqrt{n}: an across-time-bin noise floor at 2α2/(T−1)\sqrt{2\alpha^2/(T{-}1)} kicks in once within-bin sampling is adequate, and at high SEM the [0,1][0,1] clipping of α\alpha introduces a mean bias. This is treated separately in Appendix §A.6.

3. Extension 1: pinning the wtw_t column

3.1 The wtw_t cells of McFarland’s literal estimators

The wtw_t column of the §1.5 table reads directly off the literal forms.

CtotalC_\text{total}, the LHS of the LOTC (1), is the sample variance of Yi(t)Y^i(t) pooled over all (i,t)(i,t). With each sample contributing one unit of weight, bin tt contributes ntn_t units; the implicit across-bin weighting is trial-count wt∝ntw_t \propto n_t.

CpsthC_\text{psth} uses (6) — the cross-trial average of distinct-pair products at the same time bin. McFarland’s nested bracket ⟨⟨⋅⟩i≠j⟩t\langle\langle\,\cdot\,\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t averages within tt first and then averages bins uniformly: every bin contributes wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T regardless of how many pairs it contains. The Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor is the global mean over (i,t)(i, t) — trial-count wt∝ntw_t \propto n_t.

CrateC_\text{rate} uses (8) — the same nested bracket restricted to close pairs Δeij<ε\Delta e_{ij} < \varepsilon. Same reading: MM at wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T, Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor at trial-count.

Three different across-bin weightings across the three estimators, and an additional MM-vs-Y‾\bar Y split inside each of CpsthC_\text{psth} and CrateC_\text{rate}. Under (A1) constant ntn_t all four cells coincide (trial-count and 1/T1/T collapse to a constant under scaling) and the inconsistency is invisible. Under variable ntn_t they do not: the LOTC (1) fails term-by-term because the three estimators no longer measure variance/covariance under the same across-bin distribution.

3.2 Two consistent directions: uniform 1/T1/T and pair-count ∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2

Restoring term-by-term consistency means pinning the entire wtw_t column — every MM and every Y‾\bar Y subtractor in every estimator — to a single across-bin weighting. Two principled choices are available.

Uniform direction (wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T). Match McFarland’s literal MM reading: each fixation-aligned bin contributes equally regardless of ntn_t. The Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor (and CtotalC_\text{total}) are recomputed as 1T∑t1nt∑iYi(t)\frac{1}{T}\sum_t \frac{1}{n_t}\sum_i Y^i(t) — within-bin mean first, then uniform across bins. The resulting estimator answers “how does the variance behave averaged equally over the analysis time bins” — the natural reading of the LOTC LHS for a fixation-aligned analysis.

Pair-count direction (wt∝nt(nt−1)/2w_t \propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2). Pool all distinct same-bin pairs across tt into a single flat list and average uniformly per pair. Under uniform eye sampling, bin tt contributes nt(nt−1)/2n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 close pairs, so its implicit across-bin weight is ∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2. The Y‾Y‾⊤\bar Y\bar Y^\top subtractor and CtotalC_\text{total} are recomputed with the same per-bin total weight ∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2.

Both directions are consistent — every cell of the wtw_t column lands on a single value, and the LOTC (1) holds term-by-term. They differ in efficiency. The per-bin close-pair second-moment estimator has sampling variance ∝1/|Pt|\propto 1/|P_t| where |Pt|≈nt(nt−1)/2⋅Pr[Δe<ε]|P_t| \approx n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 \cdot \Pr[\Delta e<\varepsilon]; the inverse-variance-optimal across-bin combination weights bin tt by |Pt||P_t| — exactly pair-count. The uniform direction gives every bin equal voice, the natural reading of “average across analysis time bins”, but pays a variance penalty when ntn_t varies sharply because low-ntn_t bins enter with the same weight as high-ntn_t bins despite their much noisier per-bin estimates.

The same bias/variance tradeoff appears on the qq axis in §4.5: Direction 1 (full pp) is the natural target but its unbounded 1/p1/p close-pair weights are noisy in the periphery; Direction 2 (central p2p^2) uses bounded weights but reports a value shifted toward the close-pair density.

3.3 Synthetic validation and headline choice

The truth 1−α1-\alpha is invariant under time-bin weighting in the unified rate field (10): the envelope factor 𝔼w[α2]\mathbb E_w[\alpha^2] cancels in the ratio. Both consistent directions therefore target the same closed-form 1−αp1-\alpha^p. Pair the staircase ntn_t (15→36015\to360 across T=100T=100 bins) with a decaying envelope α(t)\alpha(t) that concentrates amplitude in early high-ntn_t bins (Fig. 1A) — the case that maximally separates the two directions in finite samples.

Panel B confirms that the truth is wtw_t-invariant and that both directions hit it — the difference between the two histograms is variance, not bias. Panel C extends to a continuous ℓ/σ\ell/\sigma sweep: at every spatial scale, uniform’s across-seed SD exceeds pair-count’s. The gap is driven by low-ntn_t late bins entering uniform with the same weight as high-ntn_t early bins despite their noisier per-bin contribution.

We headline the pair-count direction for all reported 1−α1-\alpha values, with one explicit caveat: pair-count concentrates the close-pair signal on early high-ntn_t bins, so the reported value leans on the early-trial portion of each fixation. The uniform direction is the more natural reading of “average over fixation-aligned time”, but its finite-sample variance under variable ntn_t is too high to use as the primary report. The same asymmetry appears on the qq axis (§4.5): Direction 1 (full pp) is headlined because the population bias relative to the actual-viewing target is small, accepting modest variance cost.

3.4 The shuffle-null fix in production

A useful diagnostic for any wtw_t mismatch: under a shuffle null that destroys the eye–spike coupling (random eye trajectories), the close-pair estimator has no real eye dependence to exploit and CrateshufC_\text{rate}^{\text{shuf}} should converge to CpsthC_\text{psth} at the same wtw_t. If CrateC_\text{rate} and CpsthC_\text{psth} are computed with different wtw_t, then Dz=fz(Cnoisecorr)−fz(Cnoiseuncorr)D_z = f_z(C_\text{noise}^{\text{corr}}) - f_z(C_\text{noise}^{\text{uncorr}}) deviates from zero under the null purely because of the weighting mismatch — independent of any (A2) violation, and independent of which direction is chosen as long as CrateC_\text{rate} and CpsthC_\text{psth} both use it.

This is the bias diagnosed and fixed in ryan/fig2/bias_diagnosis/ (Dzshuf:−0.0068→+0.0010D_z^{\text{shuf}}: -0.0068 \to +0.0010, p:<10−4→0.44p: <10^{-4}\to 0.44, after consistent pair-count weighting). The pre-fix production pipeline lived in a partially-pinned state — close-pair MM at pair-count (pool-then-average), CpsthC_\text{psth} at uniform 1/T1/T, Y‾\bar Y at trial-count — that the post-fix pipeline cleaned by pinning all three to the pair-count direction. §6.1 records the production state.

3.5 What the correction does and does not address

Extension 1 is only about the weighting across time bins. It is a no-op when the two directions coincide — either by constant ntn_t (the intersection with (A1); §A.4) or by an envelope α(t)\alpha(t) uncorrelated with ntn_t. It does not address Extension 2’s eye-position distribution mismatch, which acts within time bins on the choice of ee used to average the rate variance. The two are orthogonal: Extension 1 fills the wtw_t column of the §1.5 table; Extension 2 fills the qq column.

4. Extension 2: pinning the qq column

Section 3 filled the wtw_t column of the §1.5 table by pinning all three estimators to a single across-bin weighting. This section fills the qq column by pinning all three to a single across-eye distribution. The two extensions are independent — §3 addressed (A1) failure (variable ntn_t); §4 addresses (A2) failure (non-homogeneous stimulus) — and the same natural-vs-stable tradeoff appears on this axis: McFarland’s literal MM is pinned to the close-pair conditional density p2p^2, the actual viewing distribution is pp, and §4.5 develops both consistent directions and their bias/variance tradeoff.

4.1 Close pairs are sampled from p(e)2p(e)^2

Take two trials with eye positions ei,eje_i, e_j drawn independently from pp. The density of close pairs at position ee — pairs with ei≈ej≈ee_i\approx e_j \approx e within the threshold ball — is proportional to p(e)⋅p(e)=p(e)2p(e)\cdot p(e) = p(e)^2. Hence the close-pair estimator (7) does not measure 𝔼e∼p[r2]\mathbb{E}_{e\sim p}[r^2] but

⟨YciYcj∣Δe<ε⟩→𝔼e∼p2[rc(t,e)2](ε→0),p2(e)≡p(e)2∫p(e′)2de′. \big\langle Y_c^i Y_c^j \mid \Delta e<\varepsilon\big\rangle \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \mathbb{E}_{e\sim p^2}\!\big[r_c(t,e)^2\big] \quad (\varepsilon\to0), \qquad p^2(e) \equiv \frac{p(e)^2}{\int p(e')^2\,de'} . \tag{12}

For an isotropic Gaussian fixation p=𝒩(0,σ2I)p=\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I) the close-pair distribution is exactly p2=𝒩(0,σ22I)p^2=\mathcal N\!\big(0,\tfrac{\sigma^2}{2}I\big): a tighter, more central Gaussian with half the variance. Figure 2 confirms this geometrically and numerically and shows how Varp[F]\mathrm{Var}_{p}[F] and Varp2[F]\mathrm{Var}_{p^2}[F] differ across profiles.

4.2 The decomposition is consistent only on one distribution

Combining §1 and §4.1: in the non-homogeneous case the naive pipeline measures

Ctotal,Cpsthover p(e),Crateover p(e)2, C_{\text{total}},\,C_{\text{psth}} \ \text{over } p(e), \qquad C_{\text{rate}} \ \text{over } p(e)^2 ,

so the derived quantities mix two distributions,

1−α=1−Cpsth(p)Crate(p2),Cnoisecorr=Ctotal(p)−Crate(p2). 1-\alpha = 1 - \frac{C_{\text{psth}}(p)}{C_{\text{rate}}(p^2)}, \qquad C_{\text{noise}}^{\text{corr}} = C_{\text{total}}(p) - C_{\text{rate}}(p^2). \tag{13}

The LOTC (1) holds term-by-term only when every term is taken over the same distribution. Equation (13) violates this whenever 𝔼p2[r2]≠𝔼p[r2]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r^2]\neq\mathbb{E}_p[r^2], i.e. whenever the rate depends on absolute eye position — precisely the non-homogeneous case.

4.3 A sharper inconsistency in the naive estimator

There is a second, subtler defect already present in (7) as written. The close-pair second moment is over p2p^2, but the term subtracted from it — Y‾c2\bar Y_c^2, the global mean — is over pp:

(Crate)ccnaive=𝔼p2[rc2]⏟2nd moment over p2−(𝔼p[rc])2⏟mean over p. \big(C_{\text{rate}}\big)_{cc}^{\text{naive}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r_c^2]}_{\text{2nd moment over }p^2} - \underbrace{\big(\mathbb{E}_{p}[r_c]\big)^2}_{\text{mean over }p}. \tag{14}

This is not a variance under any single distribution. Because 𝔼p2[r]>𝔼p[r]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r]>\mathbb{E}_p[r] for a centrally-peaked profile and 𝔼p2[r]<𝔼p[r]\mathbb{E}_{p^2}[r]<\mathbb{E}_p[r] for an eccentric one, the naive CrateC_{\text{rate}} can be inflated above the true p2p^2 variance (central cells) or driven negative (eccentric cells). We confirm both below.

Note that this is a different axis from Extension 1: Extension 1’s shuffle null is blind to (A2) because shuffling eye trajectories across trials destroys the eye–spike coupling, removing exactly the r(e)r(e) dependence on which the distribution mismatch acts. A shuffle-null Dz≈0D_z\approx 0 therefore says nothing about eye-position consistency.

4.4 The corrected estimator: target distribution as a parameter

The close-pair conditioning is required to cancel the independent Poisson noise, so we cannot avoid sampling from p2p^2 when forming the second-moment estimate. But the close pairs give Poisson-free local estimates of r(t,e)2r(t,e)^2; the bias lives entirely in how those local estimates are aggregated over ee. We re-weight toward a chosen target distribution q(e)q(e) by importance sampling. A sample drawn from pp contributes weight q(e)/p(e)q(e)/p(e); a close pair drawn from p2p^2 contributes weight q(e)/p(e)2q(e)/p(e)^2:

𝔼q[g]̂=∑pairsq(e)p(e)2g∑pairsq(e)p(e)2(second moment),𝔼q[h]̂=∑samplesq(e)p(e)h∑samplesq(e)p(e)(mean, total, PSTH). \widehat{\mathbb{E}_q[g]} = \frac{\sum_{\text{pairs}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)^2}\, g}{\sum_{\text{pairs}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)^2}} \quad\text{(second moment)}, \qquad \widehat{\mathbb{E}_q[h]} = \frac{\sum_{\text{samples}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)}\, h}{\sum_{\text{samples}} \tfrac{q(e)}{p(e)}} \quad\text{(mean, total, PSTH)} . \tag{15}

Two choices of qq make all terms consistent (module estimators.py, target=):

target qq close-pair weight q/p2q/p^2 sample weight q/pq/p character
Direction 1 (full) pp 1/p1/p (unbounded) 11 actual viewing distribution
Direction 2 (central) p2p^2 11 ∝p\propto p (bounded) central; close-pair-supported
(naive) — 11 on p2p^2 11 (mean over pp) inconsistent (Eq. 14)

Both directions fix the mean/second-moment inconsistency of (14) by construction. They are the two consistent resolutions of the mismatch: push everything out to the full distribution, or pull everything in to the close-pair distribution.

Equivalence to eye-position stratification. Importance reweighting (15) is equivalent to stratifying by absolute eye position: partition ee into strata, estimate the close-pair second moment within each stratum (where p≈p\approx const so p2≈pp^2\approx p locally and the local estimate is unbiased for 𝔼[r2∣s]\mathbb E[r^2\mid s]), then aggregate strata with weights equal to the target occupancy q(s)q(s). As the strata shrink this is exactly (15); with finite strata it pools pairs before weighting, trading a little resolution for lower variance. This is the natural generalization of McFarland et al.: their estimator conditions on time bin tt and on Δe≈0\Delta e\approx0; the correction conditions additionally on absolute eye position.

4.5 Direction 1 vs Direction 2: tradeoff and fixation-scale spatial-structure measure

The two consistent directions are not equally easy to estimate. Direction 1 (target pp) requires the unbounded weight 1/p1/p, largest in the periphery — exactly where close pairs are rarest. For an eccentric-modulated cell whose variance lives in the periphery, this makes the estimate noisy; its across-seed standard deviation grows as the threshold shrinks. Direction 2 (target p2p^2) uses bounded weights ∝p\propto p, largest at the center where close pairs are abundant, and is markedly more stable.

  • Direction 1 (pp) is the scientifically natural target. The Fano factor, noise correlation, and FEM fraction are properties of the neuron under the actual viewing conditions, whose eye-position distribution is pp. A quantity reported over p2p^2 is “as if the animal fixated more tightly than it did,” which would require a caveat on every number. We therefore headline Direction 1 for any reported values.
  • Direction 2 (p2p^2) is the stable cross-check. Where Direction 1 is too noisy (eccentric cells, small thresholds, little data), Direction 2’s bounded weights give a reliable, if differently-targeted, estimate.

The gap |(1−α)full−(1−α)central|\lvert(1-\alpha)_{\text{full}} - (1-\alpha)_{\text{central}}\rvert is itself informative — but what it measures is not whether (A2) holds. The unified random-field model under (A2) (flat mask) gives a closed-form gap

(1−αp)−(1−αp2)=σ2ℓ2(ℓ2+2σ2)(ℓ2+σ2), (1{-}\alpha^p) - (1{-}\alpha^{p^2}) = \frac{\sigma^2\,\ell^2}{(\ell^2 + 2\sigma^2)(\ell^2 + \sigma^2)}, \tag{16}

which is non-zero for finite ℓ\ell, vanishing only as ℓ→0\ell\to 0 (decorrelated rates, all FEM under every DD) or ℓ→∞\ell\to\infty (uniform field, no FEM). The maximum is ≈0.17\approx 0.17 at ℓ/σ≈1.18\ell/\sigma\approx 1.18 (Fig. 0D). The gap therefore measures whether the cell’s rate has spatial structure on the fixation scale: small gap when the rate is essentially constant within a fixation (ℓ≫σ\ell\gg\sigma) or essentially decorrelated within a fixation (ℓ≪σ\ell\ll\sigma); large gap when the rate’s spatial scale and the fixation scale are comparable. Non-homogeneous masks (central, eccentric, linear) add to this baseline; Fig. 4C plots the empirical gap across mask kinds with the (A2) baseline overlaid.

The gap is still a useful empirical signal — it tells you that the choice of eye-position distribution DD matters for the reported 1−α1-\alpha — but it is not a clean test of (A2).

5. Consequences on synthetic and real data

5.1 The naive estimator’s 1−α1-\alpha failure (synthetic)

Pure-Poisson synthetic data (true noise correlation 00, true Fano 11) under the §2 unified generator, with the three non-homogeneous masks, exposes the naive estimator’s bias most clearly on 1−α1-\alpha (Fig. 3A):

  • 1−α1-\alpha (panel A): the naive estimator over-states for central masks (close pairs over-represent the center, where M2M^2 is large) and under-states for eccentric masks — occasionally producing Crate≈0C_\text{rate} \approx 0 that clips 1−α1-\alpha to 00. The matched estimator (target pp) lands on the identity line within seed noise.

The sign rule is set by where each cell’s stimulus visibility lives: the close-pair density p2(e)p^2(e) emphasizes the center, so a mask whose Ep2[M2]/Ep[M2]>1E_{p^2}[M^2]/E_p[M^2]>1 (a central mask) over-states CrateC_\text{rate} and a mask whose ratio is <1<1 (an eccentric mask) under-states it.

The leak of this 1−α1-\alpha bias into the noise correlation and Fano factor is qualitatively present but quantitatively mild under the unified model (Fig. 3B–C: naive median |r|=0.015|r|=0.015 vs matched 0.0100.010; both Fano medians ≈1.01\approx 1.01). The additive model in earlier drafts predicted much larger leaks because the additive bF(e)bF(e) term shifted the mean of rr with ee, generating a large cross-term in Y‾2\bar Y^2. In the unified multiplicative model the mean 𝔼t[r(t,e)]=μ0\mathbb E_t[r(t,e)] = \mu_0 stays ee-independent, so the cross-term vanishes in expectation and only the M2M^2-ratio bias survives. The real-data findings in §5.2 (small population shifts on Fano, 0.846→0.8750.846\to 0.875) are consistent with this milder regime.

The matched estimator recovers each target’s own closed-form decomposition (test_estimators.py::test_full_target_recovers_p_decomposition and ::test_central_target_recovers_p2_decomposition), up to a small finite-threshold smoothing that shrinks as the threshold shrinks. Figure 4 illustrates the recovery and the Direction-1/Direction-2 tradeoff.

5.2 Real-data consequences (fixRSVP, cache-only)

We applied the matched estimator to the real fixRSVP recordings, cache-only (no GPU, no model inference; generate_realdata.py reads the Figure-4 cache of trial-aligned spikes and real eye trajectories). 1−α1-\alpha and the Fano factor are computed on the real spikes with each cell’s own validity mask (reproducing the Figure-2 per-cell 1−α1-\alpha at the median); the eye-position density is a Gaussian KDE of the measured fixational positions. Pooled over 397 good cells (ccmax>0.85\mathrm{cc}_{\max}>0.85, 2 monkeys, 24 sessions):

quantity naive Direction 1 (pp, full) Direction 2 (p2p^2, central)
median 1−α1-\alpha 0.734 0.702 0.608
median Fano 0.846 0.875 —
  • The naive bias on population 1−α1-\alpha is small. The naive median (0.734) reproduces the Figure-2 value (0.732) and lies only +0.022+0.022 above the Direction-1-corrected value (0.702). On the actual-viewing target pp, the existing Figure-2 / Figure-4 panel-D 1−α1-\alpha conclusions are therefore robust to the (A2) distribution mismatch at the population level — real V1 cells do not behave like the extreme synthetic central/eccentric profiles, which suffer large biases.
  • The gap measures fixation-scale spatial structure, and it is measurable. The gap between the two consistent targets, |(1−α)full−(1−α)central|\lvert(1-\alpha)_{\text{full}} - (1-\alpha)_{\text{central}}\rvert, has a population median of 0.089 with a tail beyond 0.30.3 (Fig. 5B). Under the unified random-field model the gap is non-zero under (A2) itself when ℓ∼σ\ell\sim\sigma (Eq. 16) — peaking at ≈0.17\approx 0.17 — so gap =0.089= 0.089 is evidence that the cells’ rate maps have spatial structure on the fixation scale, which is expected for any cell with a finite spatial RF, not necessarily that (A2) is violated. What the gap does say is that the choice of eye-position distribution DD matters for the reported 1−α1-\alpha at this scale: a ±0.09\pm 0.09 swing between Direction 1 and Direction 2 is the order of the Direction-1-vs-naive bias we found above (−0.022-0.022).
  • The Fano factor shifts modestly under matching (median 0.846→0.8750.846\to0.875, +3%+3\%), consistent with the synthetic prediction that the Fano factor inherits the rate-variance distribution mismatch (Fig. 3C); per-cell shifts are larger.

The noise-correlation consequence on real spikes is small at the population level — both on the unified synthetic (Fig. 3B: naive vs matched median |r||r| differ by ≈0.005\approx 0.005) and consistent with the modest Fano shift here (0.846→0.8750.846\to 0.875). Quantifying it directly on real spike pairs requires either the full windowed pipeline or careful joint-pair validity masking; because it shares the same pipeline change, it is folded into the gated Figure-2 fix (§6) rather than approximated here.

6. The current pipeline: state and proposed production change

The covariance machinery and caches are shared by Figures 2–4, so a pipeline change has broad blast radius. This section consolidates the production-pipeline state for each extension.

6.1 Extension 1 — already integrated

Consistent pair-count time-bin weighting has been integrated into the production pipeline (VisionCore/covariance.py):

  • estimate_rate_covariance — pair-count-weighted Y‾\bar Y matching the close-pair second moment, pinning the Y‾\bar Y cell of the §1.5 table to the pair-count direction (§3.2).
  • bagged_split_half_psth_covariance — weighting parameter, default 'pair_count', pinning CpsthC_\text{psth} to the pair-count direction.

Together with the trial-count CtotalC_\text{total} already being recomputed at the same per-sample weight inside the production estimator, this pins the full wtw_t column to the pair-count direction (§3.2).

On the Allen 2022-04-13 session (49 cells, 3667 windows) the fix moved the shuffle-null DzD_z from −0.0068-0.0068 (p<10−4p<10^{-4}) to +0.0010+0.0010 (p=0.44p=0.44); the real-data DzD_z shifted only −0.0855→−0.0819-0.0855 \to -0.0819 (signal-to-null ratio 12.5×→83.7×12.5\times \to 83.7\times), so the scientific conclusion was preserved. Diagnosis and validation are recorded in ryan/fig2/bias_diagnosis/FINAL_REPORT.md.

6.2 Extension 2 — implemented here, production gated

Eye-position-distribution matching is implemented and TDD-validated in this folder:

  • estimators.decompose(target=...) — naive reproduces the existing pipeline; full is Direction 1 (the actual-viewing pp); central is Direction 2 (p2p^2).
  • test_estimators.py — 11 tests covering correctness (recovery of the closed-form decompositions under each target, finite-threshold-bias shrinkage), stability (Direction 2 stabler than Direction 1 for eccentric cells), Poisson cancellation (Fano →1\to 1), the pipeline-match (naive ↔︎ pipeline_one_minus_alpha), and Extension 1 (variable-ntn_t uniform vs pair-count weighting).

The proposed production change is to add the target argument to estimate_rate_covariance (close-pair weight q/p2q/p^2) and a corresponding per-sample weight q/pq/p to bagged_split_half_psth_covariance and the CtotalC_{\text{total}} computation in VisionCore/covariance.py, defaulted to 'naive' so the current numbers are preserved unless target='full' is explicitly requested.

The regeneration of the Figure-2 decomposition cache requires a GPU and is expensive; it is gated on explicit approval and is not performed by this note. The expected direction of the change on 1−α1-\alpha is set by §5.2: small at the population median, larger for the heavy-tail non-homogeneous cells.

Appendix: derivations

A.1 Close-pair density is p(e)2p(e)^2

Let ei,ej∼iidpe_i,e_j\stackrel{\text{iid}}{\sim}p. The probability that a pair is “close” with both members in a small ball Bδ(e)B_\delta(e) around ee is Pr[ei∈Bδ(e)]Pr[ej∈Bδ(e)]≈(p(e)|Bδ|)2\Pr[e_i\in B_\delta(e)]\,\Pr[e_j\in B_\delta(e)] \approx \big(p(e)\, |B_\delta|\big)^2. Dividing by the total close-pair mass and taking δ→0\delta\to0 gives the close-pair position density p(e)2/∫p2p(e)^2/\int p^2. For p=𝒩(0,σ2I)p=\mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I) in dd dimensions, p(e)2∝exp(−∥e∥2/σ2)=𝒩(0,σ22I)p(e)^2 \propto \exp(-\lVert e\rVert^2/\sigma^2)=\mathcal N(0,\tfrac{\sigma^2}{2}I) up to normalization — variance halved.

A.2 Importance weights

To estimate 𝔼q[g]=∫gq\mathbb E_q[g] = \int g\,q from samples distributed as ss, write 𝔼q[g]=∫gqss=𝔼s[gqs]\mathbb E_q[g] = \int g\,\tfrac{q}{s}\,s = \mathbb E_s\! \big[g\,\tfrac{q}{s}\big], so each sample is weighted by q/sq/s. For the second moment the close pairs have s=p2s=p^2, giving weight q/p2q/p^2 (Direction 1: p/p2=1/pp/p^2=1/p; Direction 2: p2/p2=1p^2/p^2=1). For the mean/total/PSTH the trials have s=ps=p, giving weight q/pq/p (Direction 1: 11; Direction 2: pp). Substituting into the empirical LOTC terms yields a decomposition in which all of CtotalC_{\text{total}}, CpsthC_{\text{psth}} and CrateC_{\text{rate}} are taken over the single distribution qq, restoring term-by-term consistency of (1).

A.3 Two readings of the nested-bracket close-pair estimator

The close-pair second-moment estimator (7) is written ⟨⟨YciYcj∣Δe<ε⟩i≠j⟩t\langle\langle Y_c^iY_c^j \mid \Delta e<\varepsilon\rangle_{i\ne j}\rangle_t. Two readings of the nested bracket give two consistent across-bin weightings.

Inner-then-outer (uniform 1/T1/T). Read the brackets literally as two stages: at each bin tt, average the close-pair products to get a per-bin mean m‾t=|Pt|−1∑(i,j)∈PtYiYj\bar m_t = |P_t|^{-1}\sum_{(i,j)\in P_t} Y^iY^j; then average the {m‾t}\{\bar m_t\} uniformly across the TT bins. Each bin contributes wt=1/Tw_t = 1/T regardless of |Pt||P_t|. This is the natural reading of “averaging across time points” — McFarland’s text on p.6228.

Pool-then-average (pair-count ∝nt(nt−1)/2\propto n_t(n_t{-}1)/2). Pool all close pairs from all bins into a single flat list and average uniformly per pair. Bin tt then contributes |Pt|/∑t′|Pt′||P_t|/\sum_{t'}|P_{t'}|, which under uniform eye sampling is ≈nt(nt−1)/2\approx n_t(n_t{-}1)/2 normalized.

Under constant ntn_t the two readings coincide (|Pt||P_t| is the same for every tt). Under variable ntn_t they differ — the bin with the most close pairs either gets the same voice as every other (uniform) or dominates the average (pair-count). Both are consistent across-bin weightings for the LOTC, and §3 develops the bias/variance tradeoff between them.

A.4 When the corrections are a no-op

  • Extension 1. If ntn_t is constant across time bins, the uniform and pair-count directions coincide (the two readings of §A.3 give the same weight per bin), and Extension 1 reduces to the identity.
  • Extension 2. The correction is the identity whenever (A2) holds at the second moment: 𝔼t[r2(t,e)]\mathbb E_t[r^2(t,e)] independent of ee makes 𝔼e∼D[𝔼t[r2]]\mathbb E_{e\sim D}\!\big[\mathbb E_t[r^2]\big] the same for every DD, so the close-pair second moment converges to the right Vartotal\mathrm{Var}_\text{total} regardless of its p2p^2 sampling. This reproduces McFarland’s stated regime. Note however that even under (A2), VarPSTHD\mathrm{Var}_\text{PSTH}^D does depend on DD through the eye- distribution-spread of the PSTH integrand, so the Direction-1 and Direction-2 targets do not coincide in 1−α1-\alpha; their gap is the fixation-scale spatial-structure measure of §4.5. The test_homogeneous_mask_correction_is_noop_for_full_target test confirms that under (A2) (flat mask), target='naive' and target='full' agree on 1−αp1-\alpha^p while target='central' recovers 1−αp21-\alpha^{p^2}.

A.5 Closed-form decomposition for the unified rate field

With the unified rate equation (9), the across-time-bin and across-eye distributions decouple by linearity. Write Xt(e)=M(e)α(t)st(e)X_t(e) = M(e)\,\alpha(t)\, s_t(e), so r=μ0+Xr = \mu_0 + X. The field is zero-mean, 𝔼[st(e)]=0\mathbb E[s_t(e)] = 0, with covariance K(δ)=τ2exp(−∥δ∥2/(2ℓ2))K(\delta) = \tau^2\exp(-\lVert\delta\rVert^2/(2\ell^2)) and independent draws across time bins.

Mean. 𝔼t,e∼D[r]=μ0\mathbb E_{t,e\sim D}[r] = \mu_0 for any w,Dw, D (the field is zero-mean and the mean is constant in ee).

Total variance. VartotalD,w=𝔼[X2]=𝔼w[α2]𝔼D[M2]𝔼s[st(e)2]=𝔼w[α2]τ2𝔼D[M2]\mathrm{Var}_\text{total}^{D,w} = \mathbb E[X^2] = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,\mathbb E_D[M^2]\,\mathbb E_s[s_t(e)^2] = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,\tau^2\,\mathbb E_D[M^2].

PSTH variance. Let GtD=𝔼e∼D[M(e)st(e)]G_t^D = \mathbb E_{e\sim D}[M(e)\,s_t(e)]. Then 𝔼e∼D[rt(e)]=μ0+α(t)GtD\mathbb E_{e\sim D}[r_t(e)] = \mu_0 + \alpha(t)\,G_t^D and

GtDG_t^D is i.i.d. across time bins with 𝔼[GtD]=0\mathbb E[G_t^D] = 0 and 𝔼[(GtD)2]=IM,K,D\mathbb E[(G_t^D)^2] = I_{M,K,D} (see below). In the large-TT limit

VarPSTHD,w=𝔼w[α2]IM,K,D, \mathrm{Var}_\text{PSTH}^{D,w} = \mathbb E_w[\alpha^2]\,I_{M,K,D},

where

IM,K,D=∬M(e1)M(e2)K(e1−e2)D(e1)D(e2)de1de2. I_{M,K,D} = \iint M(e_1)\,M(e_2)\,K(e_1{-}e_2)\,D(e_1)\,D(e_2)\,de_1\,de_2.

For the flat mask (M≡1M \equiv 1) the integral reduces to 𝔼u[K(u)]\mathbb E_u[K(u)] with u=e1−e2u = e_1 - e_2. If D=𝒩(0,σ2I)D = \mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I) in ℝ2\mathbb R^2, then u∼𝒩(0,2σ2I)u \sim \mathcal N(0, 2\sigma^2 I) and the Gaussian-Gaussian integral evaluates to

𝔼u∼𝒩(0,2σ2I)[τ2e−∥u∥2/(2ℓ2)]=τ2⋅ℓ2ℓ2+2σ2. \mathbb E_{u\sim\mathcal N(0, 2\sigma^2 I)}\!\big[\tau^2 e^{-\|u\|^2/(2\ell^2)}\big] = \tau^2 \cdot \frac{\ell^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma^2}.

giving the flat mask closed forms

1−αp=2σ2ℓ2+2σ2,1−αp2=σ2ℓ2+σ2, 1-\alpha^p = \frac{2\sigma^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma^2}, \qquad 1-\alpha^{p^2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{\ell^2 + \sigma^2},

and the (A2)-respecting gap

(1−αp)−(1−αp2)=σ2ℓ2(ℓ2+2σ2)(ℓ2+σ2). (1{-}\alpha^p) - (1{-}\alpha^{p^2}) = \frac{\sigma^2\,\ell^2}{(\ell^2 + 2\sigma^2)(\ell^2 + \sigma^2)}.

The maximum is at ℓ/σ=21/2≈1.19\ell/\sigma = \sqrt{2}^{1/2} \approx 1.19 (taking the derivative in ℓ2\ell^2 and setting to zero).

For the central mask M(e)=exp(−∥e∥2/(2ℓM2))M(e) = \exp(-\lVert e\rVert^2/(2\ell_M^2)), the products M(e1)M(e2)D(e1)D(e2)M(e_1)M(e_2)D(e_1)D(e_2) are still Gaussian; the integral reduces to a Gaussian-Gaussian convolution with effective σM2=σ2ℓM2/(σ2+ℓM2)\sigma_M^2 = \sigma^2\ell_M^2/(\sigma^2 + \ell_M^2):

IM,K,p=τ2⋅(ℓM2σ2+ℓM2)2⋅ℓ2ℓ2+2σM2. I_{M,K,p} = \tau^2 \cdot \left(\frac{\ell_M^2}{\sigma^2 + \ell_M^2}\right)^{2} \cdot \frac{\ell^2}{\ell^2 + 2\sigma_M^2}.

For eccentric and linear masks the integrands are Gaussian-times-bounded-functions; the closed forms exist but are tedious, and we use Monte Carlo (4M-sample default) via synthetic.ground_truth(...), with sampling noise ≲10−3\lesssim 10^{-3} — well below the test tolerances.

The ratio 1−αD,w=1−IM,K,D/(τ2𝔼D[M2])1-\alpha^{D,w} = 1 - I_{M,K,D}/(\tau^2 \mathbb E_D[M^2]) is invariant under time-bin weighting and the envelope α(t)\alpha(t): both VarPSTH\mathrm{Var}_\text{PSTH} and Vartotal\mathrm{Var}_\text{total} are proportional to 𝔼w[α2]\mathbb E_w[\alpha^2], which cancels. Both Extension-1 directions therefore target the same closed-form ratio; the choice between them is a finite-sample efficiency question, not a bias question.

A.6 Consistency: how sd[1−α̂]\mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] depends on NN and TT, and the [0,1][0,1] clipping bias

Section 2.4 noted that the seed-to-seed SEM of McFarland’s estimator does not shrink as 1/N1/\sqrt{N} alone — an across-time-bin noise floor in TT kicks in once within-bin sampling is adequate, and at high SEM the [0,1][0,1] clipping of α̂\hat\alpha introduces a mean bias. This appendix derives both effects on the unified flat-mask synthetic under (A1)+(A2) and calibrates them empirically against the closed form. The code is fig_consistency.py; the empirical sweep is cached to consistency_sweep.npz.

A.6.1 The across-time-bin floor

Take the flat mask (M≡1M\equiv 1), constant nt=Nn_t = N, and deterministic rates (no observation noise). The per-time-bin PSTH is 𝔼e∼p[r(t,e)]=μ0+Gt\mathbb E_{e\sim p}[r(t,e)] = \mu_0 + G_t with

Gt=∫st(e)p(e)de. G_t = \int s_t(e)\,p(e)\,de.

By (A2) the {Gt}t=1T\{G_t\}_{t=1}^{T} are i.i.d. zero-mean across time bins with

Vp≡𝔼Gt2=∬K(e1−e2)p(e1)p(e2)de1de2=τ2ℓ2ℓ2+2σ2=α*τ2,α*≡ℓ2ℓ2+2σ2. V_p \;\equiv\; \mathbb E\,G_t^2 \;=\; \iint K(e_1-e_2)\,p(e_1)\,p(e_2)\,de_1\,de_2 \;=\; \frac{\tau^2\,\ell^2}{\ell^2+2\sigma^2} \;=\; \alpha^*\,\tau^2, \qquad \alpha^* \equiv \frac{\ell^2}{\ell^2+2\sigma^2}.

In the large-NN limit the close-pair pool (∼T⋅N(N−1)/2\sim T\cdot N(N{-}1)/2 pairs) makes Ĉrate→τ2\widehat C_{\text{rate}}\to \tau^2 deterministically, so the estimator collapses to

α̂≈1τ2⋅1T−1∑t(Gt−G‾)2, \hat\alpha \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\tau^2}\cdot\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_t\!(G_t-\bar G)^2,

the sample variance of TT i.i.d. Gaussians divided by their (assumed known) population variance scaling. Its sampling variance is

Var[α̂]=1τ4⋅2Vp2T−1=2α*2T−1, \mathrm{Var}[\hat\alpha] \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau^4}\cdot\frac{2\,V_p^2}{T-1} \;=\; \frac{2\,{\alpha^*}^2}{T-1},

giving the across-time-bin SEM floor

limN→∞sd[1−α̂]=α*2T−1, \lim_{N\to\infty} \mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] \;=\; \alpha^*\,\sqrt{\frac{2}{T-1}}, \tag{A6.1}

a finite limit in TT alone. At ℓ=σ\ell=\sigma (so α*=1/3\alpha^*=1/3) and T=100T=100, (A6.1) gives sd≈0.0474\mathrm{sd}\approx 0.0474, in agreement with the leveling-off observed in §2.4.

A.6.2 Within-bin contribution and the empirical decomposition

At finite NN, the close-pair estimator’s local fluctuations and the finite-trial sampling of GtG_t each contribute a within-bin noise term that scales as 1/N1/\sqrt N at fixed TT. Treating the across-time-bin and within-bin contributions as approximately independent,

sd[1−α̂]≈sdwithin2(N)+sdfloor2(T), \mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] \;\approx\; \sqrt{\;\mathrm{sd}_{\text{within}}^2(N) \;+\; \mathrm{sd}_{\text{floor}}^2(T)\;}, \tag{A6.2}

so adding trials drives the SD down toward the floor but does not pierce it. Fig. A6A shows the heatmap of empirical sd[1−α̂]\mathrm{sd}[1-\hat\alpha] over (N,T)(N,T) at ℓ=σ\ell=\sigma: the right edge (large TT, sd≈0.04\mathrm{sd}\approx 0.04) is dominated by the floor; the left edge (small TT) is dominated by the floor as well — across the whole grid the floor sets the bottom of the achievable SD. Fig. A6C shows the same data sliced at fixed NN: the N=800N=800 points sit on the analytical floor curve α*2/(T−1)\alpha^*\sqrt{2/(T-1)} at large TT and rise above it at small TT where within-bin noise becomes significant relative to the floor.

The practical implication is that for a target SEM on 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha, more time bins is the only binding knob once trials/bin is large enough to drop within-bin noise below the floor. In fixRSVP with T∼100T\sim 100 post-fix bins, α*=1/3\alpha^*=1/3 gives a floor of ≈0.047\approx 0.047, comparable to the cross-cell variability of the population 1−α1-\alpha estimate.

The §2.3 stimulus-frame caveat applies here: in real fixRSVP adjacent analysis bins inside a single 2020 Hz stimulus-frame interval share the same field draw, so the effective TT for (A6.1) is #fixations×#stim-frames per fixation\#\text{fixations}\times\#\text{stim-frames per fixation} rather than the raw bin count. With a 5050 ms stimulus-frame period and ∼6\sim 6–1212 post-fix bins per fixation (depending on bin width), the effective TT is reduced by the bins-per-frame multiplicity and the floor correspondingly rises. We do not propagate this correction through the real-data numbers in §5.2 — those are robust to it at the population median (Δ 1−α=−0.0221-\alpha = -0.022) — but it matters for per-cell SEM and is the operative reason the within-stimulus-frame reliability question is flagged as a future direction (§2.3).

A.6.3 Boundary-clipping bias

estimators.py:252 clips α̂\hat\alpha to [0,1][0,1] before reporting 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha. The clip is sensible — α\alpha is a fraction and the ratio of two variance estimates can excursion outside — but it introduces a bias on the mean whenever the sampling SD σα\sigma_\alpha is comparable to the distance from α*\alpha^* to the nearest boundary.

Treating α̂∼𝒩(α*,σα2)\hat\alpha\sim\mathcal N(\alpha^*, \sigma_\alpha^2) and clipping to [0,1][0,1], the post-clip mean is the truncated-Gaussian moment

𝔼[clip(α̂)]=α*[Φ(z1)−Φ(z0)]+σα[φ(z0)−φ(z1)]+[1−Φ(z1)], \mathbb E\!\left[\mathrm{clip}(\hat\alpha)\right] = \alpha^*\bigl[\Phi(z_1)-\Phi(z_0)\bigr] \;+\; \sigma_\alpha\bigl[\varphi(z_0)-\varphi(z_1)\bigr] \;+\; \bigl[1-\Phi(z_1)\bigr], \tag{A6.3}

with z0=−α*/σαz_0 = -\alpha^*/\sigma_\alpha, z1=(1−α*)/σαz_1 = (1-\alpha^*)/\sigma_\alpha, and φ,Φ\varphi,\Phi the standard normal pdf/cdf. The sign of the bias on 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha is set by which boundary is closer:

  • Small ℓ\ell (α*→0\alpha^*\to 0, 1−α*→11-\alpha^*\to 1): the lower tail of α̂\hat\alpha is clipped up to 00, so 𝔼[clip(α̂)]>α*\mathbb E[\mathrm{clip}(\hat\alpha)]>\alpha^* and 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha is biased down.
  • Large ℓ\ell (α*→1\alpha^*\to 1, 1−α*→01-\alpha^*\to 0): the upper tail of α̂\hat\alpha is clipped down to 11, so 𝔼[clip(α̂)]<α*\mathbb E[\mathrm{clip}(\hat\alpha)]<\alpha^* and 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha is biased up.

Both pull 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha toward the interior 1/21/2. Fig. A6B verifies (A6.3) on the small-ℓ\ell side: at ℓ=0.3σ\ell=0.3\sigma (α*=0.043\alpha^*=0.043), each (N,T)(N,T) cell produces an (sd,bias)(\mathrm{sd},\mathrm{bias}) pair, and the empirical points sit on the analytical curve as σα\sigma_\alpha is varied. The bias reaches ∼−0.08\sim -0.08 at σα≈0.3\sigma_\alpha\approx 0.3 — small in absolute terms, but a 9%9\% shift relative to the true 1−α*=0.9571-\alpha^*=0.957. The same effect explains the saturation of 1−α̂1-\hat\alpha at 00 for eccentric-mask cells in §5.1, where the unclipped close-pair α̂\hat\alpha excursions above 11 from the inflated-CrateC_\text{rate} side of the naive inconsistency.

Reproduce all figures and tests (from this folder):

uv run python fig_mechanism.py
uv run python fig_sanity_check.py
uv run python fig_time_bin_weighting.py
uv run python fig_naive_failure.py
uv run python fig_correction.py
uv run python fig_consistency.py            # parallel sweep; cached to .npz
uv run python generate_realdata.py          # cache-only; --recompute to rebuild
uv run --with pytest pytest test_estimators.py -q

Build this note to a self-contained, offline HTML (math via MathML, images inlined):

pandoc writeup.md -s --mathml --self-contained -o writeup.html
